verses.text: Hiram sends cedars and builders for David's house, and David perceives the LORD has established him as king 'for his people Israel's sake.' He reads his rise rightly — God's gift, given for others' good, not his own glory.

Hiram sends cedars and builders for David's house, and David perceives the LORD has established him as king 'for his people Israel's sake.' He reads his rise rightly — God's gift, given for others' good, not his own glory.

David takes more wives in Jerusalem and many children are born, among them Nathan and Solomon. The note records both blessing and a quiet flaw, for the law had warned the king against many wives. Grace and human weakness run side by side.

When the Philistines attack, David 'enquired of God' before fighting, wins at Baal-perazim, then burns their abandoned idols. The new king asks before he acts. Unlike Saul, David seeks the LORD, and ascribes the breakthrough to God.

When the Philistines attack, David 'enquired of God' before fighting, wins at Baal-perazim, then burns their abandoned idols. The new king asks before he acts. Unlike Saul, David seeks the LORD, and ascribes the breakthrough to God.

When the Philistines return, David enquires again, and God gives a new plan — wait for the sound in the mulberry trees, 'for God is gone forth before thee.' He does not presume on yesterday's answer. Fresh obedience seeks fresh guidance, and God goes before His people.
